title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Miss Jane Marple sits by her window in Saint Mary Mead, observing her garden. Due to physical limitations, she can only perform light pruning and must rely on Mr. Larkin to manage the gardening. While Mr. Larkin agrees to Miss Marple's specific requests regarding planting columbines and bellflowers, he habitually ignores her instructions in favor of his own preferences, such as planting excessive amounts of cabbage and kale or prioritizing his favorite flowers like asters and lavender. He often uses the weather or other tasks as excuses for his inaction.

Miss Marple reflects on the changing nature of Saint Mary Mead, noting that while the village's core—including the Blue Boar, the church, and her own historic house—remains, the character of the town has shifted due to modern developments. She observes that shops are becoming more modern, such as the fishmonger with its frozen displays, though Mr. Barnes’s grocery store remains traditional. Miss Hartnell expresses frustration with modern packaging and the inconvenience of new shopping habits.

As Miss Marple knits, she contemplates the recent murder of the Colonel, described as a boring and unpleasant man, and thinks of his widow, Greta Prothero, a loyal friend. She also notes the physical changes to the landscape, specifically the new housing developments referred to as "alleys." Finally, she reminisces about the era of domestic servants like Florence and the maids from St. Fés, contrasting their uneducated but practical skills with the modern trend of educated women and foreign students performing housework.
